expanded_description:
The world's economies are often classified into two broad categories: developed (advanced, industrialized, high-income) and developing (emerging, low-income, less developed). Developed economies are characterized by high income per capita, advanced technology, well-established institutions, high levels of human capital, and diversified economic structures. Developing economies are characterized by lower income per capita, less advanced technology, weaker institutions, lower levels of human capital, and often a heavy reliance on agriculture or natural resources. However, the distinction is not binary; there is a continuum from the poorest countries (e.g., Burundi, South Sudan) to the richest (e.g., Norway, Switzerland, Singapore), with many countries in between (e.g., China, India, Brazil, Mexico) classified as emerging or middle-income economies.

Understanding the characteristics and differences between developed and developing economies is essential for analyzing global economic inequality, evaluating development policies, and assessing the prospects for economic convergence. The gap between rich and poor countries is vast: GDP per capita in developed countries exceeds \$50,000 (PPP-adjusted), while in the poorest countries it is below \$2,000. This gap has persisted for decades, though some countries (China, South Korea, India) have made significant progress.

In this section, we will define developed and developing economies, examine key indicators (income, health, education, technology, institutions), analyze the structural differences between the two groups, discuss the diversity within developing countries (low-income, lower-middle, upper-middle, emerging), and explore the concept of the 'development trap.' You will learn to classify countries by income level, interpret human development indicators, and understand the multiple dimensions of underdevelopment.

core_concept.principle:
Developed and developing economies differ along multiple dimensions: income per capita, health, education, technology, institutions, and economic structure. The World Bank classifies countries by GNI per capita (Atlas method).

World Bank Income Classifications (2024):

• Low-income economies: GNI per capita < \$1,135
• Lower-middle-income economies: GNI per capita \$1,136 - \$4,465
• Upper-middle-income economies: GNI per capita \$4,466 - \$13,845
• High-income economies (developed): GNI per capita > \$13,845

Key Characteristics of Developed Economies:

• High GDP per capita (> \$30,000, often > \$50,000 PPP)
• High life expectancy (> 80 years)
• Low infant mortality (< 5 per 1,000)
• High literacy and education attainment (> 95% literacy, > 12 years schooling)
• Advanced technology and infrastructure
• Strong institutions (property rights, rule of law, low corruption)
• Diversified economy (services dominate, manufacturing advanced, agriculture small)
• Stable political systems

Key Characteristics of Developing Economies:

• Low to middle GDP per capita (< \$13,845, often < \$5,000)
• Lower life expectancy (60-75 years)
• Higher infant mortality (> 20 per 1,000)
• Lower literacy and education attainment (60-90% literacy, 5-10 years schooling)
• Less advanced technology and infrastructure
• Weaker institutions (corruption, weak property rights)
• Less diversified economy (agriculture large share, informal sector large)
• Often political instability

United Nations Human Development Index (HDI):

Combines income, life expectancy, and education into a single index (0 to 1).

• Very high human development: HDI > 0.800 (developed)
• High human development: HDI 0.700-0.799
• Medium human development: HDI 0.550-0.699
• Low human development: HDI < 0.550

Other Classifications:

• Emerging economies: Rapidly growing middle-income countries (BRICS: Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa; also Mexico, Turkey, Indonesia, Vietnam).
• Least developed countries (LDCs): Poorest, most vulnerable countries (46 countries, mainly in Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia).
• OECD members: Mostly developed countries (38 members).
• G7/G20: Major economies.

The Development Trap:

Many developing countries remain poor due to a combination of factors: low saving (poverty trap), poor health (disease burden), low education (low human capital), weak institutions (corruption, conflict), and geography (landlocked, tropical diseases). Escaping the trap requires coordinated policies across multiple dimensions.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two countries: Norway and Niger. Norway's GDP per capita is about \$80,000; Niger's is about \$600. Life expectancy in Norway is 83 years; in Niger it is 62 years. Literacy in Norway is near 100%; in Niger it is about 35%. Norway has strong institutions, advanced technology, and a diversified economy. Niger has weak institutions, low technology, and an economy dependent on subsistence agriculture. The gap is enormous.

But not all developing countries are equally poor. China's GDP per capita is about \$12,000 (upper-middle income), India's is about \$2,500 (lower-middle income). China has made remarkable progress in reducing poverty (from 88% in 1981 to less than 1% today). India has also made progress but lags behind China.

The World Bank classifies countries by income. Low-income countries (e.g., Afghanistan, Somalia, Yemen) have GNI per capita below \$1,135. Lower-middle-income countries (e.g., India, Nigeria, Vietnam) have GNI between \$1,136 and \$4,465. Upper-middle-income countries (e.g., China, Brazil, Mexico) have GNI between \$4,466 and \$13,845. High-income countries (e.g., US, Canada, Germany, Japan) have GNI above \$13,845.

The Human Development Index (HDI) provides a broader measure of development, combining income, health (life expectancy), and education (years of schooling). Canada's HDI is 0.936 (very high); India's is 0.633 (medium); Niger's is 0.400 (low).

The development trap refers to the vicious cycle of poverty: low income leads to low saving, which leads to low investment, which leads to low productivity, which leads to low income. Escaping the trap requires a 'big push' or coordinated policies in health, education, infrastructure, and institutions.

For Canada, as a developed country, development issues focus on maintaining productivity growth, addressing inequality, and providing foreign aid to developing countries.

image.alt: World map showing countries colored by income level (World Bank classification): high-income (blue), upper-middle (green), lower-middle (yellow), low-income (red).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.1: World map of countries by income level. High-income countries (developed) are concentrated in North America, Europe, Australia, and parts of Asia; low-income countries are concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of Asia.

sections.introduction:
The world's economies are often classified into two broad categories: developed (advanced, industrialized, high-income) and developing (emerging, low-income, less developed). Developed economies are characterized by high income per capita, advanced technology, well-established institutions, high levels of human capital, and diversified economic structures. Developing economies are characterized by lower income per capita, less advanced technology, weaker institutions, lower levels of human capital, and often a heavy reliance on agriculture or natural resources. However, the distinction is not binary; there is a continuum from the poorest countries (e.g., Burundi, South Sudan) to the richest (e.g., Norway, Switzerland, Singapore), with many countries in between (e.g., China, India, Brazil, Mexico) classified as emerging or middle-income economies.

Understanding the characteristics and differences between developed and developing economies is essential for analyzing global economic inequality, evaluating development policies, and assessing the prospects for economic convergence. The gap between rich and poor countries is vast: GDP per capita in developed countries exceeds \$50,000 (PPP-adjusted), while in the poorest countries it is below \$2,000. This gap has persisted for decades, though some countries (China, South Korea, India) have made significant progress.

In this section, we will define developed and developing economies, examine key indicators (income, health, education, technology, institutions), analyze the structural differences between the two groups, discuss the diversity within developing countries (low-income, lower-middle, upper-middle, emerging), and explore the concept of the 'development trap.'

introduction_image.alt: Histogram of countries by GDP per capita (PPP); bimodal distribution shows cluster of poor countries and cluster of rich countries, with fewer in between.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 14.1.2: The distribution of countries by GDP per capita is bimodal: a cluster of poor countries (mostly Sub-Saharan Africa) and a cluster of rich countries (OECD), with fewer countries in the middle.

key_concept.definition:
Developed Economy: A country with high income per capita, advanced technology, strong institutions, high human capital, and diversified economic structure.

Developing Economy: A country with lower income per capita, less advanced technology, weaker institutions, lower human capital, and often heavy reliance on agriculture or natural resources.

World Bank Income Classifications (2024, GNI per capita, Atlas method):

• Low-income: < \$1,135
• Lower-middle-income: \$1,136 - \$4,465
• Upper-middle-income: \$4,466 - \$13,845
• High-income: > \$13,845

United Nations Human Development Index (HDI):

Composite index of: life expectancy (health), education (mean years of schooling, expected years), and GNI per capita (income).

• Very high human development: HDI > 0.800
• High human development: HDI 0.700-0.799
• Medium human development: HDI 0.550-0.699
• Low human development: HDI < 0.550

Key Characteristics Compared:

| Indicator | Developed | Developing |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| GDP per capita (PPP) | > \$30,000 | < \$13,845 (often < \$5,000) |
| Life expectancy | > 80 years | 60-75 years |
| Infant mortality (per 1,000) | < 5 | > 20 (often > 50) |
| Literacy rate | > 95% | 60-90% |
| Mean years of schooling | > 12 | 5-10 |
| Agriculture share of GDP | < 2% | 15-30% |
| Services share of GDP | > 70% | 40-60% |
| Informal economy share | < 20% | > 40% |
| Corruption perception | Low | High |

Other Classifications:

• Emerging economies (emerging markets): Rapidly growing middle-income countries (BRICS, Mexico, Turkey, Indonesia, Vietnam).
• Least developed countries (LDCs): 46 countries with low income, low human capital, and economic vulnerability (UN classification).
• OECD members: 38 mostly developed countries.
• G7/G20: Major economies.

The Development Trap (Poverty Trap):

Low income → low saving → low investment → low capital → low productivity → low income (vicious cycle).

Escaping the trap requires a 'big push' or coordinated policies: health (reduce disease), education (build human capital), infrastructure (roads, electricity), institutions (property rights, rule of law), and trade (access to markets).
key_concept.context: The classification of countries into developed and developing has a long history. The term 'Third World' emerged during the Cold War (First World: US and allies; Second World: Soviet bloc; Third World: non-aligned, mostly poor). Today, the World Bank's income classification is most commonly used. The Human Development Index was introduced by the UNDP in 1990 as a broader measure of development beyond income. The concept of the 'poverty trap' was popularized by Jeffrey Sachs (2005) and others. For Canada, as a developed country, development issues are addressed through foreign aid, trade policy, and participation in international institutions (World Bank, IMF, UN).

image.alt: World map showing HDI by country: very high (dark blue), high (light blue), medium (yellow), low (red).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.3: Human Development Index (HDI) map. Developed countries have very high HDI; developing countries range from high to low HDI.

classifications.value: The World Bank classifies countries by GNI per capita into four income groups: low, lower-middle, upper-middle, and high
characteristics: Low-income (LIC): GNI per capita < \$1,135 (e.g., Afghanistan, Somalia, Yemen, Burundi).
High-income (HIC): > \$13,845 (e.g., US, Canada, Germany, Japan, Australia).
Classification is updated annually based on GNI per capita (Atlas method).
Countries can move between categories over time (e.g., China moved from low to upper-middle).
classifications.behavior:
The World Bank's income classification is the most widely used measure of development. It is simple and data-driven, but it has limitations: it does not capture health, education, or inequality. A country could have high average income but high inequality, leaving many people poor (e.g., South Africa). It also does not capture non-income dimensions of well-being.

Countries can move between categories. China was low-income in 1980; it is now upper-middle-income. India is lower-middle-income, moving toward upper-middle. Many Sub-Saharan African countries remain low-income, though some (e.g., Ghana, Rwanda) have moved to lower-middle.

The classification is used for aid eligibility: the World Bank's International Development Association (IDA) provides concessional loans to low-income countries. The International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) lends to middle-income countries.

For Canada (high-income), development policy focuses on aid to low-income countries, trade agreements, and participation in global institutions.

image.alt: Table: World Bank income classification thresholds (2024) with example countries in each category.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.4: World Bank income classification thresholds and example countries. China has moved from low to upper-middle income; India is lower-middle; many African countries remain low-income.

examples.explanation: In 1980, China's GNI per capita was about \$220 (low-income). Through rapid growth, it reached lower-middle in the 1990s, upper-middle in the 2000s, and is now approaching high-income (\$12,000). This transition has lifted over 800 million people out of poverty.

Many Sub-Saharan African countries have remained low-income for decades (e.g., Malawi, Burundi, Niger). Low saving, high population growth, poor health, weak institutions, and conflict have kept them poor. Some countries (e.g., Ghana, Rwanda, Ethiopia) have moved to lower-middle income.

classifications.value: The HDI is a composite measure of development that includes income, health, and education, providing a broader picture than income alone
characteristics: Three dimensions: health (life expectancy), education (mean years of schooling, expected years), income (GNI per capita, PPP).
Very high human development: HDI > 0.800 (developed countries).
High human development: HDI 0.700-0.799 (many emerging economies).
Medium human development: HDI 0.550-0.699 (most developing countries).
classifications.behavior:
The Human Development Index (HDI) was created by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) to provide a broader measure of development than income alone. It captures the idea that development is about people's capabilities—to live long, healthy lives, to be educated, and to have a decent standard of living.

Comparing HDI and income per capita reveals interesting patterns. Some countries have higher HDI than their income would suggest (e.g., Costa Rica, Sri Lanka, Cuba) due to good health and education policies. Others have lower HDI than income would suggest (e.g., South Africa, Brazil) due to inequality or poor health/education outcomes.

Canada's HDI is 0.936 (very high), ranking 15th in the world. Canada scores well on life expectancy (82 years), education (13 years mean schooling), and income (\$50,000 PPP).

Low HDI countries are concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa (e.g., Niger, Chad, Central African Republic). These countries face multiple challenges: low income, low life expectancy (due to disease, malnutrition), and low education (due to lack of schools, child labor).

image.alt: Diagram: HDI components (health, education, income) and how they are combined into the index.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.5: The Human Development Index combines health (life expectancy), education (mean and expected years of schooling), and income (GNI per capita) into a single measure.

examples.explanation: Canada's HDI is 0.936 (very high). Life expectancy: 82.5 years. Mean years of schooling: 13.2. Expected years: 16.4. GNI per capita (PPP): \$50,000. Canada ranks 15th in the world, behind Norway, Switzerland, Ireland, and Australia.

Niger's HDI is 0.400 (low). Life expectancy: 62 years. Mean years of schooling: 2.1. Expected years: 6.5. GNI per capita: \$1,200. Niger ranks last (189th) in the world. It faces extreme poverty, high child mortality, and low education.

classifications.value: Developed and developing economies differ in economic structure, technology, institutions, and informality
characteristics: Agriculture share: Developed < 2% of GDP; Developing 15-30%.
Informal economy: Developed < 20% of employment; Developing > 40% (often > 70% in low-income countries).
Technology: Developed (advanced, high R&D); Developing (less advanced, technology adoption).
Institutions: Developed (strong property rights, low corruption); Developing (weaker, higher corruption).
Infrastructure: Developed (good roads, electricity, internet); Developing (poor, unreliable).
classifications.behavior:
Structural transformation is the shift of economic activity from agriculture to industry to services. Developed countries have completed this transformation: agriculture is a small share of GDP (often < 2%), industry is moderate (20-25%), and services dominate (> 70%). Developing countries are at earlier stages: agriculture still employs a large share of the population (though its share of GDP is falling), industry is growing, and services are expanding.

The informal economy is a defining feature of developing countries. Informal workers are not registered, pay no taxes, and lack social protection. In low-income countries, the informal sector can account for 70-90% of employment. This reduces government revenue, limits worker protections, and perpetuates poverty.

Institutions in developed countries are strong: property rights are protected, contracts are enforced, corruption is low, and the rule of law prevails. In developing countries, institutions are weaker: corruption is common, property rights are insecure, and contract enforcement is unreliable. This deters investment and hinders growth.

For Canada, structural transformation is complete. The economy is service-dominated (70% of GDP), with a small agricultural sector (2%) and a significant industrial sector (28%, including oil and gas, manufacturing). Institutions are strong; Canada ranks low on corruption. The informal economy is small (about 10-15% of GDP).

image.alt: Bar chart: Sectoral composition of GDP for developed vs. developing countries; agriculture, industry, services.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.6: Sectoral composition of GDP: developed countries are service-dominated; developing countries have larger agriculture and industry shares.

examples.explanation: Canada's GDP is 70% services, 28% industry (including oil and gas, manufacturing, construction), and 2% agriculture. The informal economy is about 10-15% of GDP. Institutions are strong (property rights, rule of law, low corruption).

India's GDP is 50% services, 25% industry, and 15% agriculture (but agriculture employs 40% of the workforce). The informal economy is about 50% of GDP. Institutions are weaker (corruption, slow contract enforcement).

principles.principle: Development Is Multidimensional; Income Alone Is an Incomplete Measure
principles.statement: While income per capita is strongly correlated with many dimensions of well-being (health, education, life expectancy), it is not sufficient. Countries with the same income can have very different health and education outcomes (e.g., Costa Rica vs. South Africa). The Human Development Index (HDI) was created to capture these multiple dimensions. Other measures include the Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI), the Gini coefficient (inequality), and the Gender Development Index (GDI). Development is ultimately about expanding people's capabilities (Amartya Sen's capability approach). This implies that development policy should focus not only on raising income but also on improving health, education, and social inclusion.
principles.application: Policymakers in developing countries should not focus solely on GDP growth. They should also invest in health (vaccination, clean water, sanitation), education (schools, teacher training), and social protection (safety nets). International organizations (UNDP, World Bank, WHO) provide support for these non-income dimensions. For Canada, foreign aid should target health, education, and governance, not just economic growth.
principles.example: Costa Rica has income per capita of \$13,000 (upper-middle) but life expectancy of 80 years and literacy of 98%, comparable to developed countries. South Africa has income per capita of \$14,000 (upper-middle) but life expectancy of 64 years (due to HIV/AIDS) and high inequality. Income alone does not capture these differences.

image.alt: Diagram: Multiple dimensions of development (income, health, education, institutions, environment, equality).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.7: Development is multidimensional. Income alone is an incomplete measure; health, education, institutions, equality, and environment also matter.

principles.principle: The Development Trap: Poor Countries Often Stay Poor Due to Multiple Interlocking Factors
principles.statement: Many developing countries remain trapped in poverty due to a vicious cycle: low income leads to low saving, which leads to low investment, which leads to low productivity, which leads to low income. This is the poverty trap. Additional factors reinforce the trap: poor health (disease burden reduces productivity), low education (lack of human capital), weak institutions (corruption, insecurity), poor infrastructure (lack of roads, electricity), and geography (landlocked, tropical diseases). Escaping the trap requires a 'big push'—coordinated investments in multiple sectors simultaneously. This is the rationale for foreign aid: to provide the initial capital to break the trap. However, aid alone is not sufficient; good policies and institutions are also needed.
principles.application: Policies to escape the development trap include: investing in health (vaccination, malaria prevention, clean water), education (primary and secondary schooling), infrastructure (roads, electricity, internet), and institutions (property rights, rule of law, anti-corruption). Foreign aid can help, but aid must be well-targeted and accompanied by policy reforms. The Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) provide a framework for coordinated action.
principles.example: Malawi remains trapped in poverty: GDP per capita is \$600, life expectancy is 65 years, literacy is 65%. Low income leads to low saving, low investment, and low productivity. Malaria, HIV/AIDS, and malnutrition reduce productivity. Weak institutions and poor infrastructure further hinder growth. Escaping the trap requires a coordinated push in health, education, agriculture, and infrastructure.

image.alt: Diagram: Vicious cycle of poverty (low income → low saving → low investment → low productivity → low income).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.8: The poverty trap: a vicious cycle of low income, low saving, low investment, low productivity, and low income. Escaping the trap requires a 'big push' of coordinated investments.

worked_examples.title: Calculating and Interpreting the Human Development Index

image.alt: Table: Life expectancy (75 years), mean years of schooling (10), expected years (12), GNI per capita (\$10,000 PPP); dimension indices calculation; HDI calculation.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.9: Worked example: calculating the Human Development Index from health, education, and income data.

worked_examples.problem:
A country has the following data:

• Life expectancy: 75 years
• Mean years of schooling: 10 years
• Expected years of schooling: 12 years
• GNI per capita (PPP): \$10,000

HDI formulas:
• Health index = (Life expectancy - 20) / (85 - 20)
• Education index = (Mean years index + Expected years index) / 2
Mean years index = (Mean years - 0) / (15 - 0)
Expected years index = (Expected years - 0) / (18 - 0)
• Income index = [ln(GNI) - ln(100)] / [ln(75,000) - ln(100)]
• HDI = (I_Health × I_Education × I_Income)^{1/3}

a) Calculate the health index.
b) Calculate the mean years index and expected years index, then the education index.
c) Calculate the income index.
d) Calculate the HDI.
e) Interpret the HDI: is this country in the very high, high, medium, or low human development category?

step_by_step.explanation:
Mean years index = (10 - 0) / (15 - 0) = 10 / 15 = 0.6667
Expected years index = (12 - 0) / (18 - 0) = 12 / 18 = 0.6667
Education index = (0.6667 + 0.6667) / 2 = 1.3334 / 2 = 0.6667

HDI = 0.732 falls in the high human development category (0.700-0.799). This country has good health, decent education, and moderate income, but is not yet at the very high level of developed countries (HDI > 0.800). Countries with similar HDI include Brazil, Mexico, China, and Turkey.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 0.8462. b) Education index = 0.6667. c) Income index = 0.6956. d) HDI = 0.732. e) High human development (0.700-0.799).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Human Development Index formulas to calculate a country's HDI and interpret its development level.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Countries by Income and Development Stage

image.alt: Table: Country A (GNI \$50,000, HDI 0.94), Country B (GNI \$12,000, HDI 0.78), Country C (GNI \$2,500, HDI 0.65), Country D (GNI \$800, HDI 0.45).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.10: Worked example: classifying countries by World Bank income group and UNDP HDI category.

worked_examples.problem:
Four countries have the following data:

| Country | GNI per capita (USD) | HDI |
|---------|---------------------|-----|
| A | 50,000 | 0.94 |
| B | 12,000 | 0.78 |
| C | 2,500 | 0.65 |
| D | 800 | 0.45 |

World Bank thresholds (2024):
• Low-income: < \$1,135
• Lower-middle: \$1,136-\$4,465
• Upper-middle: \$4,466-\$13,845
• High-income: > \$13,845

UNDP HDI categories:
• Very high: > 0.800
• High: 0.700-0.799
• Medium: 0.550-0.699
• Low: < 0.550

a) Classify each country by World Bank income group.
b) Classify each country by UNDP HDI category.
c) Is there a perfect correlation between income group and HDI category? Explain.
d) Which country has the largest 'gap' between income and HDI? What might explain this?
e) Which country would be eligible for IDA concessional loans?

step_by_step.explanation:
Country A: \$50,000 > \$13,845 → High-income
Country B: \$12,000 (between \$4,466 and \$13,845) → Upper-middle-income
Country C: \$2,500 (between \$1,136 and \$4,465) → Lower-middle-income
Country D: \$800 < \$1,135 → Low-income

Country A: 0.94 > 0.800 → Very high human development
Country B: 0.78 (0.700-0.799) → High human development
Country C: 0.65 (0.550-0.699) → Medium human development
Country D: 0.45 < 0.550 → Low human development

There is a strong positive correlation between income and HDI, but it is not perfect. In this example, all countries fall into corresponding categories (high-income with very high HDI, etc.). However, in reality, some countries have higher HDI than their income would suggest (e.g., Costa Rica, Sri Lanka) and some have lower HDI than income would suggest (e.g., South Africa, Brazil).

step_by_step.step: Largest gap between income and HDI
step_by_step.explanation: In this example, there is no gap (all match). In reality, we would look for countries where HDI is significantly higher or lower than predicted by income. For instance, Cuba has HDI of 0.78 (high) but income of \$9,000 (upper-middle), which is higher than expected. South Africa has HDI of 0.71 (high) but income of \$14,000 (upper-middle), which is lower than expected due to inequality and HIV/AIDS.

The World Bank's International Development Association (IDA) provides concessional loans to low-income countries (GNI < \$1,135) and some lower-middle-income countries that are IDA-eligible based on creditworthiness. Country D (low-income) would be eligible for IDA loans. Country C (lower-middle) might be eligible if it meets creditworthiness criteria (some lower-middle countries are IDA-eligible, e.g., India, Nigeria).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) A: High-income, B: Upper-middle, C: Lower-middle, D: Low-income. b) A: Very high HDI, B: High HDI, C: Medium HDI, D: Low HDI. c) Strong correlation but not perfect; some countries have higher/lower HDI than income predicts. d) In this example, none; in reality, Cuba (higher) and South Africa (lower). e) Country D (low-income) is IDA-eligible; Country C may be eligible depending on creditworthiness.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying World Bank income classification and UNDP HDI categories to classify countries and understand the relationship between income and broader development.

process_flow.process: Collect data on income, health, education, and institutions
process_flow.description: Obtain GNI per capita (World Bank), life expectancy (WHO), education (UNESCO), HDI (UNDP), corruption indices (Transparency International).
process_flow.mechanism: World Bank Open Data, UNDP Human Development Reports, WHO Global Health Observatory.

image.alt: Diagram: Data sources (World Bank, UNDP, WHO, UNESCO) → indicators.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.11: Step 1 - Collect data on income, health, education, and institutions from international organizations.

process_flow.process: Classify country by income (World Bank thresholds)
process_flow.description: Use World Bank GNI per capita thresholds to assign income group.

image.alt: Decision tree: GNI per capita → income group.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.12: Step 2 - Classify the country by income using World Bank thresholds.

process_flow.description: Calculate or look up HDI and assign category.

image.caption: Figure 14.1.13: Step 3 - Classify the country by Human Development Index category.

process_flow.process: Assess structural characteristics (sectoral composition, informality, institutions)
process_flow.description: Examine the economy's structure: agriculture share, services share, informal economy size, corruption perception.
process_flow.mechanism: Agriculture share of GDP (World Bank); informal economy size (ILO estimates); Corruption Perception Index (Transparency International).

image.alt: Diagram: Structural indicators (agriculture share, informality, corruption).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.14: Step 4 - Assess structural characteristics: sectoral composition, informality, and institutional quality.

process_flow.process: Identify development traps and policy priorities
process_flow.description: Determine whether the country faces a poverty trap and what policies are needed to escape.
process_flow.mechanism: If low income, low saving, low investment, poor health, low education → poverty trap. Policies: health, education, infrastructure, institutions.

image.alt: Diagram: Identify poverty trap → policy priorities (health, education, infrastructure, institutions).
image.caption: Figure 14.1.15: Step 5 - Identify development traps and prioritize policies to escape them.

image.alt: Master framework: Collect data → classify by income → classify by HDI → assess structure → identify traps → prioritize policies.
image.caption: Figure 14.1.16: Complete framework for analyzing developed vs. developing economies.

summary: Developed and developing economies differ along multiple dimensions: income per capita, health, education, technology, institutions, and economic structure. The World Bank classifies countries by GNI per capita into low-income (< \$1,135), lower-middle (\$1,136-\$4,465), upper-middle (\$4,466-\$13,845), and high-income (> \$13,845). The United Nations Human Development Index (HDI) combines income, life expectancy, and education into a single measure, capturing broader dimensions of development. Developed countries are characterized by high income, high life expectancy (>80 years), high literacy (>95%), strong institutions, and service-dominated economies. Developing countries have lower income, lower life expectancy, lower education, weaker institutions, and larger agricultural and informal sectors. The development trap (poverty trap) is a vicious cycle: low income → low saving → low investment → low productivity → low income. Escaping the trap requires a 'big push' of coordinated investments in health, education, infrastructure, and institutions. Understanding the characteristics and differences between developed and developing economies is essential for analyzing global inequality, evaluating development policies, and assessing the prospects for economic convergence.
